story:
The town is, was, and will be. It stands where it has always stood, older than any memory and tougher than any storm. Its houses lean toward a cold, murky river; its ancient oaks keep their silent watch; its doors creak and its timbers sigh. Of all its peculiar people, none were stranger than the withered old man on the highest hill. In a creaky house with broken windows and a porch that sagged, he had rocked through more years than anyone could count. No one knew where he came from or how long he had lived. They only knew he paid for his rare supplies with gold coins no one recognized, stamped with kings no book remembered.

The town did not change. Storms ripped the forest, yet the streets stood. A month of rain swelled the river until it crawled over the banks, then slid back, as if embarrassed by its own boldness. Children played on the shores until their mothers called them, and the river carried the same gray silt past the same weary stones. Even on the day the river coughed up an ancient talisman beside the church, and a curious boy brought it to the preacher, the town went on. The preacher—respected by day, restless by night—took it home. In secret he built a small altar. He did not speak of it, and the town did not ask. If that choice was unwise, the town made no judgment, only kept on breathing its stubborn, steady breath.

Months later, at a late hour when the world breathes shallow, a crack snapped across the earth. The sound hung too long, like a note that would not end. From that fissure rose swarms of winged shapes, hot wind gusting from their pig-like snouts. Huge, leathery, webbed wings beat the air. Their rounded, gelatinous bodies lifted, veined and glistening, eyes glowing red along the sky in a number too great to count. In one motion, unified and intent, the devil-things rose and turned toward the town that never changes.

The sleeping town had already begun to dream of them: dark wings, downward mouths, downward hands. A scream cut the night. Doors flew open. People rushed into the streets and saw what froze their souls—the preacher in the grip of those snouted horrors, torn and devoured in great, sickening bites. When the last quarter had gone down, the creatures turned their rimmed eyes on the crowd. With a grunting snuffle, they rushed. Many monsters seized many villagers. Together they lifted into the night and returned to the crack. Two gigantic hands reached up from below and pulled the earth closed, smooth as if nothing had ever opened there.

Back in the town, nothing moved but the things that had always moved: the river flowing, the doors whispering, the oaks breathing in their slow way. To a passerby it would seem mostly unchanged, though the silence lay heavier than before. The old man on the hill sat on his porch, rocking, watching, keeping a lonely vigil over what was his world and had always been his world. The town is, and was, and shall be, forevermore.
